speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tuesday, January the 30th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing the Reverend Dr. Isaac Levy. We're in London, England. And this is tape 4. Dr. Levy, would you like to introduce your wife, Tonie, to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Tonie-- this is the lady whom I married in 1937, who, throughout my-- our married life has had to bear the burden of my problems and frustrations. And particularly during the war when I was abroad, life was far from easy. She had one little boy to worry about, to look after, and concerned, of course, for my welfare.

speaker: interviewer
text: But particularly when I was in Germany and I used to write letters virtually every day to keep her informed of what I was doing and was taking her into my confidence because I had no one else that I could convey to. She had to bear that-- the burden. How do you react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one had to bear more than that burden. One was aware all the time what was happening in the world. And knowing the suffering that was taking place in England, I was also aware that everywhere in the world, women, particularly, were suffering. Because their children were involved not in a glorious adventure, but in something that would end with their death. And after a while, we'd realized there would be no victory for anybody. And that was the most dreadful thing. Worrying about my husband was a natural thing. But I was much more concerned with the events that were making him so absolutely involved in everything except his own life. And I was terribly anxious that he should be aware that he had to be strong physically in order to cope with all the worries that he was handling day by day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like receiving these letters from him with the most horrendous things in them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I had been receiving letters from the Middle East for a very long time. So we were used to news about conquests and failures. But getting news from Germany was a totally different thing. It was disastrous for everybody. I was living in the bosom of my family-- my parents were alive. And they waited as eagerly as I did for news. And part of my anxiety was that they should not know how dreadful things were.

speaker: interviewer
text: That your parents you shouldn't, shouldn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: That your parents shouldn't know how dreadful things were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shouldn't be aware the day-by-day tragedies that were taking place. Now, they were not ignorant of the fact that the most dreadful bestiality was happening all over the world. But they had lived through a history of martyrdom and of being aware of the problems of our people. And it seemed to me, being so much younger, that they couldn't take it. In actual fact, in the final analysis, they buoyed us up. Because they realized that things would pass, and life would return to normal, and perhaps we would even gain by it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to point out, you know-- I talked to you before about frustration. Tonie used to receive my letters. But she didn't just read them, she also transfers the information on those letters to other people. Now you can tell them what happened when you approached--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I approached everybody I knew. And I was terribly involved during the war. I was fairly busy in-- on different stratas of society. But with one particular friend who had an entree into government circles, I actually gave him my letters. And he took them round. And he waited for more information to come. And as it arrived, I gave it to him. The sad thing was that when my husband returned to England for what was supposed to be leave-- and instead became an opportunity to tell people face to face what was happening-- many of the people who had read these letters said quite openly they had never heard of these things. They didn't believe it was possible that such atrocities should take place. That was the thing that really, I think, broke my innocent belief that there was fundamental good in people. And I was horrified to think that the conscience of the government was a convenient one, and that it was possible for people who knew what was happening to close their minds to it. And, of course, exactly the same thing is happening now with all the tragedies that are taking place over the world. When it's expedient, the people who are supposed to run our affairs turn a blind eye to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think it was for political reasons that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --pretended not to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which they are doing now all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a certain gentleman who held supreme office in this country, who talked about the 'wailing Jews.' I shan't mention his name. He's dead now. I suppose one could. He--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was terribly charming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. So if the Jews complain, they were wailing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ultimately, of course, we heard that Hitler had been vanquished. And my husband's letters were quite-- they were quite joyous about it. And I remember writing to him that we have 'scorched the snake, not killed it.' He'll grow and be himself again. It's a quote from Shakespeare. I don't quite know where. But I have not been surprised that fascism and the Nazi doctrine has gone throughout the world. Because he wasn't vanquished in the same way as Saddam has not been vanquished. The evil lives on. And I think it's our duty, as much as we can to make that known to innocent people, and to try and fight it as much as we can. Because it's not an evil that affects only our people. It affects the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much-- or, or what effects did the whole experience have on your subsequent life together?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is hard to believe that we were known as the happy people. When ultimately I became very seriously involved in what my husband was doing, very much aware of the evil that was sort of overwhelming us, I remember my mother saying, you've lost your girlish laughter. We have lost our girlish laughter. I, I think we're happy in our way. But we are-- we have been sufficiently disillusioned about the good in the world. I don't believe in intrinsic evil. But I think, to a very large degree, evil is within our ranks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Allow me to just give you an example of this. Some years after the war, the, the television produced a series called And there was one, one episode about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some time afterwards, I was asked by TV in a short, late-night program-- to-- they interviewed me. And what effect did it have on your religious faith? There were five of us of different, different walks of life. And I said, my faith in God might not have been affected quite as much as my faith in man. And that still applies. Men can be just worse than animals in their behavior. And we-- when we talk about the, the sanctity of the human personality, this was destroyed by these people. And I said, when you saw them shoveling corpses into a mass grave, where was the sanctity of the human personality? You forgot that they were humans, almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the behavior to-- of people to people, this is the most terrible thing of all. And this is something which my wife feels very conscious about and always has done. So you can weep on each other's shoulder. But it doesn't help very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had high hopes. When we were very young, we were perfectly sure we will put the world to right. Obviously, our parents and our grandparents had made a mess of things, you know, with the First World War, which hadn't proved anything. And we were going to show what we can do. And we had-- we were a group of friends. And we were absolutely certain that the-- everything would be all right in the world. Well, I hope that somewhere-- and I'm sure that somewhere in England, in America, and other parts of the world, there are young people who feel exactly the way we did, that they will do everything to improve the conditions of, of mankind. The fact that people are completely materialistic now, of course, militates against anything like that. But I have high hopes. And we are terribly fortunate we've got grandchildren. And I think each one of them is really rather lovely in their attitude to life. And we've got a tiny, weeny little one, who is-- [LAUGHS] she's a sort of miniature girl guide-- believes [LAUGHS] in, in a good deed a day. And they're so normal. And they're so bright. And hopefully everything will be all right, finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had one son before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was a baby, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: --then I waited quite a long time. I did not want to have children. I was afraid. I really was terrified of bringing a child into an evil world. But my mother, who had seen a lot of life, said that many people who were not healthy-- women in the camp, for instance, were longing to have children. That was their one idea to become normal again. And my mother said, these people have gone through so much are going to produce children. And you, who are healthy and young, have to produce children who are ready to face the world. And I must say that I was very glad we took her advice--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because we've been very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, is there anything else you would like to, to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we've covered the ground pretty well.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a new one thing. I think my husband said-- was talking to you at one time about my father. My father was very ill, dying. And they insisted on operating on him. You know, doctors are very eager to sort of bring back hope where there isn't any hope. And on his deathbed, my father said to my husband, it will be good-- in Hebrew, [HEBREW]-- whatever happens, life will be good. And then he said, and you know, about dying, it can't be bad. No one has yet come back to grumble. [LAUGHTER] And I think the fact that he believed so much in life, even on the verge of death, has given us tremendous comfort. And I think he was right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, I agree. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
